data.title: Voya Global Perspectives Market Models - Mutual Fund Series Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Strategy overview

Globally diversified core strategy based on the Global Perspectives unique investment philosophy and built with Voya’s suite of proprietary and multi-manager mutual funds.

Key takeaways

  • In the fourth quarter of 2024, U.S. stocks continued their upward trajectory following Donald Trump’s presidential victory. The U.S. bond market struggled due to concerns about sticky inflation, increased deficit spending and a more conservative rate-cut path from the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed), which saw the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield rise by more than 80 basis points (BP) to 4.58% by the end of the quarter.
  • Global markets face challenges in 2025, but the U.S. remains attractive due to post-election optimism, investment, productivity gains and energy independence, with the Fed expected to continue gradual easing.
  • During 4Q24, the Voya Global Perspectives Market Models: MF Series delivered negative absolute and mixed relative returns.
  • On a net-of-fees basis for the following: The Voya GPMM Income Model outperformed the Bloomberg Global Aggregate Bond Index. The Voya GPMM Conservative Growth MF, Voya GPMM Moderate Growth MF and Voya GPMM Aggressive Growth MF models underperformed the S&P Target Risk Conservative, Moderate and Aggressive Indices, respectively.

Market review

U.S. stocks continued their upward trajectory during the fourth quarter following Donald Trump’s presidential victory. The consumer discretionary and communication services sectors led, while materials and healthcare lagged. Large cap stocks outperformed small caps, and growth stocks significantly beat value stocks.

The U.S. bond market struggled during the quarter on concerns about sticky inflation, increased deficit spending and the U.S. Fed’s more conservative rate-cut path, as the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield rose by more than 80 bp, ending the quarter at 4.58%. The Fed cut rates by 25 bp in November and December. However, the central bank now projects just two rate cuts in 2025, reflecting a more cautious stance in response to strong economic data.

The U.S. election, marked by Trump’s proposed tariffs, increased global uncertainty, lead to further U.S. dollar strength and broadly weighed on international equities. Europe struggled with political instability in France and Germany and rising energy costs ahead of winter. In yen terms, Japanese equities rose due to strong corporate activity including strategic mergers, share buybacks and improved return on equity. Meanwhile, China dealt with weak consumer activity, property sector challenges and concerns about regulatory changes and economic restructuring. These combined factors caused international equities to continue to underperform.

Portfolio review

The Voya GPMM MF Series posted negative absolute and mixed relative returns across models. The Income portfolio performed best from an absolute and relative standpoint.

The models were in base positioning during the period. Asset allocation was a detractor. An underweight in U.S. large cap and overweights to emerging markets (EM), real estate investment trust (REIT) and long government bonds were the main detractors. U.S. stocks delivered another period of positive performance, helped by continued strong corporate earnings. Sharply rising long-term interest rates hurt REIT and bonds. They also contributed to a stronger U.S. dollar, which weighted on EM stocks.

Manager selection was mixed. Strategies that contributed most to performance were Voya High Yield Bond, Voya Large-Cap Growth and Voya Mid Cap Research Enhanced Index. The underlying managers that detracted most were Voya Multi-Manager Emerging Markets Equity, Voya Global Bond and Principal Global Real Estate.

For the quarter ending September 30, 2024—actual company reporting finishing in December 2024—the year over year earnings growth for the S&P 500 companies was 9.1%. Overall, other fundamental market indicators do not suggest overriding the earnings signal is warranted. Therefore, the GP strategy will maintain base positioning and rebalance back to targets in early January 2025.

Outlook

Global challenges and opportunities confront the markets in 2025. However, the U.S. remains attractive driven by post-election optimism, investment, productivity gains from deregulation, innovation and energy independence. Despite some softening, the U.S. labor market remains resilient. Inflation is expected to stay manageable, allowing the Fed to continue its gradual easing.

Supported by this positive outlook and President Trump’s pro-business proposals, we continue to overweight U.S. large and mid-cap stocks. Large-cap valuations appear expensive, but they are supported by strong corporate profitability. The increasing weight of the technology sector with its capital-light business models suggest that higher margins may be the new norm, making expectations more reasonable. With the election concluded, corporate spending is expected to resume and drive positive earnings growth as well. Mid-caps offer enticing valuations and are poised to benefit from a broadening expansion and lower borrowing costs and provide more financial stability than small caps.

Outside the U.S., pockets of weakness are becoming more apparent, particularly in the eurozone and China. The eurozone faces cooling services and ongoing manufacturing challenges, heightening economic uncertainty. In China, new policies aim to address economic sluggishness, especially in the property market, and boost investor confidence. However, the investment environment looks more promising in Japan, where deflation has been curbed and corporate reforms have made progress. Despite this, there has been relatively little foreign investment, creating an opportunity as more investors recognize the growth story.

In fixed income, U.S. investment-grade core bonds offer decent yields and portfolio stability. While conviction on rates near-term direction is limited, we believe core bonds will hedge against equity risk due to the transition from inflation to growth risk that has occurred. While credit stress and defaults are expected to remain controlled, high yield spreads are very tight, leading us to prefer equities for risk allocation. In addition, an expectation that the U.S. dollar rally will continue and a positive U.S. economic outlook justify a preference for U.S. assets and an underweight in foreign currency denominated bonds.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: AVSU: Solid Value-Leaning ETF For ESG Conscious Investors
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The Avantis Responsible U.S. Equity ETF (NYSEARCA:AVSU) is designed for ESG-focused investors seeking active broad market exposure to all size segments for a reasonable price. Fund managers aim to enhance returns by emphasizing the value and quality factors, but my analysis suggests otherwise, and I couldn't find many instances where AVSU was fundamentally superior to its Russell 3000 Index benchmark or other ESG-themed funds offered by iShares. As a result, I don't think AVSU will gain much traction, so I've limited my rating on it to simple "hold." I look forward to explaining why in more detail below.

AVSU Overview

Strategy Discussion

AVSU launched on March 15, 2022, and has a 0.15% expense ratio and $300 million in assets under management. Compared to its benchmark, represented by the iShares Russell 3000 ETF (IWV), it's a pretty small fund. Other competing ETFs like the iShares ESG Aware MSCI USA ETF (ESGU), the iShares ESG MSCI USA Leaders ETF (SUSL), and the iShares MSCI USA ESG Select ETF (SUSA) had multi-year head starts on AVSU and, not surprisingly, have much larger asset bases. Of these, ESGU is easily the most successful, with $11.49 billion in AUM.

Avantis lists four reasons why you should consider AVSU on its website:

1. Gain exposure to U.S. companies across all size segments that meet specific environmental, social, and governance measures.

2. Enhance expected returns by emphasizing securities trading at lower valuations with higher profitability ratios.

3. Access an efficient portfolio management and trading process that reduces risk and transaction costs.

4. Can "fit seamlessly into an investor's asset allocation."

Avantis follows an exclusions process when selecting securities, which simply means that rather than include stocks that score well on ESG metrics, it excludes stocks that don't score well on ESG metrics. AVSU's prospectus provides some examples, such as:

  • companies with significant revenue (e.g., 5%+) derived from oil and gas production or distribution, civilian firearms, nuclear weapons, palm oil production, tobacco or cannabis, gambling, and adult entertainment
  • companies with any revenue derived from thermal coal
  • companies with no female directors
  • companies with low corporate governance scores

These are just some examples, and Avantis retains the right to exercise discretion when excluding a company. The prospectus also notes how managers primarily use a company's adjusted book-to-price ratio to define value and the cash from operations to book value ratio to define profitability. Again, managers may also rely on other value and profitability ratios, and as an actively managed fund, they can exercise discretion.

AVSU Weighting Scheme

Regarding AVSU's weighting scheme, the prospectus states the following:

To determine the weight of a security within the portfolio, the portfolio managers use the market capitalization of the security relative to that of other eligible securities as a baseline, then overweight or underweight the security based on the characteristics described above.

This language is standard for Avantis ETFs, but Avantis exaggerates how much active weighting actually is happening with AVSU. A quick calculation matching up its components' weights and market caps shows a 95.5% correlation, showing how market caps are still the primary factor behind each component's weights. For reference, here are the correlations for the other four ETFs:

  • IWV: 99.6%
  • ESGU: 97.5%
  • SUSL: 99.7%
  • SUSA: 91.4%

AVSU Composition Differences vs. Peer Group

These high correlation figures don't mean AVSU is cap-weighted like IWV. They only illustrate a positive link between size and weight, and there are still some important individual stock weight differences to consider. Using IWV's top 25 holdings as a baseline, the following table highlights these differences.

As shown, Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) is AVSU's first key exclusion, followed by Exxon Mobil (XOM) and Philip Morris (PM). Notably, AVSU includes Walmart (WMT), which the other three ESG funds exclude, so it doesn't seem like the screening process is too strict. For more aggressive ESG exposure, SUSA is the most effective, as it excludes mega-cap stocks like Amazon (AMZN) and Meta Platforms (META). However, that approach drastically changes the risk/return profile, and that's not Avantis' purpose with AVSU.

ESG funds often take more risk by overweighting Technology companies, as they typically have a low carbon footprint. However, AVSU is different. As shown below, it underweights Technology by 7.18% on average, with the primary offset in Financials, consistent with Avantis' typical "value" lean.

There are also high allocations to Consumer Discretionary stocks like The TJX Companies (TJX) and General Motors (GM), and AVSU is the only fund to have virtually no exposure to Energy. Even SUSA includes some Oil & Gas Storage & Transportation stocks like ONEOK (OKE) and Cheniere Energy (LNG).

AVSU Analysis

Performance Snapshot

Since AVSU launched on March 15, 2022, it has delivered a 13.77% total return, which was about 3-6% worse than its peers. SUSL did the best with a 20.41% gain, but that's only because of its high allocation to Nvidia (NVDA). Most will agree that its historic run-up over the last few years is unlikely to repeat, and with a 28% decline YTD, it's been one of the hardest-hit stocks since President Trump announced his sweeping global tariffs.

Since AVSU has the least exposure to Nvidia, I wouldn't place too much emphasis on this performance chart. However, I also wouldn't assume it's "safer" just because of its valuation lean. Since April 2022, AVSU's volatility, as measured by standard deviation, was the highest of the five at 19.55%, and its 19.92% maximum drawdown was only marginally better and obviously not enough to offset its underperformance in the bull market years.

Fundamental Analysis By Top 25 Holdings

The next table lists selected fundamental metrics for AVSU's top 25 holdings, which comprise 36.47% of the portfolio. Not surprisingly, this concentration level is quite low compared to its peers, highlighting Avantis' preference for designing well-diversified portfolios.

Here are two additional observations to consider:

AVSU: Solid Valuation Discount, Reasonable Growth Rates

The first thing that stands out is AVSU's attractive value ratios. For example, its 15.44x forward P/E represents a 16% relative discount to IWV and even more compared to the three ESG funds. It's a similar story for AVSU's 11.44x trailing P/CF and its 3.06x trailing P/B ratio, so there's little doubt it's a unique option for value-leaning ESG investors. You will have to sacrifice some growth potential - AVSU's historical and estimated sales and earnings growth rates are a few percentage points below, but a lot of those differences are concentrated in mega-cap tech stocks, so for me, it's a fair tradeoff.

AVSU: Below Average Quality

For the second time in as many days, I've found Avantis' quality metrics to be average at best and borderline disappointing. In the table above, I noted AVSU's weighted average ROE, ROA, and net margin figures, which were all below the peer group average. In my view, quality is the #1 factor long-term investors should focus on, and many times, value ETFs sacrifice it too much. To further demonstrate, I've also listed additional quality statistics below, and since AVSU is below average on all of them, I suggest that's the reason for its high volatility and why it hasn't performed better in drawdowns.

In addition to lower margins, AVSU's holdings are slightly more leveraged than its peer group, as evidenced by its 1.22x and 5.62x debt-equity and debt-free cash flow ratios. These figures all feed into AVSU's 8.64/10 sector-adjusted profit, which I calculated using Seeking Alpha Factor Grades, leading to the conclusion that AVSU has sacrificed some quality and growth in pursuit of value. That approach might work, but it hasn't since AVSU's inception and is not my preferred strategy.

Investment Recommendation

AVSU is a well-diversified core ESG fund with a 0.15% expense ratio and $300 million in assets under management. My analysis of its composition indicates the screening process isn't too strict, though it is one of the few ESG funds that completely avoids the Energy sector. On the other hand, some inclusions, like Walmart, are surprising, so perhaps it's not the ideal fund for "responsible" investors, despite its name.

AVSU's track record since its launch three years ago has been underwhelming because of low allocations to mega-cap growth stocks like Nvidia, but looking forward, it can turn things around if markets begin to favor value stocks. Its 15.44x forward P/E ratio represents a solid 16% discount over the Russell 3000 Index, and its lower growth rates probably might not matter much if the economy softens. Still, I was disappointed to find AVSU's holdings had below-average profit margins and above-average debt ratios, which offset the value and diversification benefits the fund offers. As a result, I don't think there's anything too special about AVSU, but it is a decent value-oriented option for ESG-conscious investors, so I've decided to rate it a neutral "hold." Thank you for reading.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 04/10/25
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: Voya MidCap Opportunities Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Strategy overview

Actively managed mid-cap growth strategy that relies on fundamental research and analysis to identify companies with strong and accelerating business momentum, increasing market acceptance and attractive valuations.

Key takeaways

• In the fourth quarter of 2024, the equity markets experienced mixed returns. Domestic large- and small-cap stocks delivered positive returns, while value indices and international markets saw declines. Cyclical sectors, such as consumer discretionary, financials and technology, outperformed defensive sectors. Growth factors led performance, while value factors declined.

• For the quarter, the Fund underperformed its benchmark, Russell Midcap Growth Index (the Index), on a net asset value (NAV) basis due to unfavorable stock selection.

• The outlook for equities in 2025 is cautiously optimistic as the U.S. economy remains strong and the Trump administration is expected to implement favorable policies in terms of deregulation and taxes. However, risks such as tariffs, inflation and geopolitics may result in volatility.

Portfolio review

U.S. stocks continued their upward trajectory during the fourth quarter following Donald with the S&P 500 Index rising by 2.41% and the Nasdaq Composite advancing by 6.17%. The consumer discretionary and communication services sectors led, while materials and healthcare lagged. Large cap stocks outperformed small caps, and growth stocks significantly beat value stocks.

The U.S. bond market struggled during the quarter on concerns about sticky inflation and cut path. The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index declined by 3.06% and the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield rose by more than 80 basis points (bp), ending the quarter at 4.58%. The Fed cut rates by 25 bp in November and December. However, the central bank now projects just two rate cuts in 2025, reflecting a more cautious stance in response to strong economic data.

For the quarter, the Fund underperformed the Index on NAV basis largely due to selection effects. The stock selection effects within the industrials, health care and consumer discretionary sectors were the greatest detractors. Alternatively stock selection within the information technology, communication services and materials sectors contributed to performance.

Key contributors to the quarter's performance were AppLovin Corp. (APP), Astera Labs, Inc. (ALAB) and Nu Holdings Ltd (NU).

An overweight position in AppLovin Corp. (APP) contributed to performance. The stock surged in November following a strong earnings report, with revenue significantly surpassing expectations. Additionally, future guidance exceeded analysts' estimates, driven by optimism around the growth of the company's artificial intelligence (AI) software.

An overweight position in Astera Labs, Inc. contributed to performance this quarter. The company reported strong third-quarter earnings, including its fifth consecutive record quarterly revenue.

Not owning Nu Holdings Ltd. (NU) contributed to performance this month. The stock declined as concerns grew over Brazil's weakening currency and rising inflation, which could pressure the company's operations.

Additionally, Nu Holdings recently undertook a significant investment in a South African digital bank, raising investor concerns about potential overextension.

Key detractors for the quarter were Monolithic Power Systems, Inc. (MPWR), Coinbase Global, Inc. (COIN) and Builders FirstSource, Inc (BLDR).

An overweight position in Monolithic Power Systems, Inc. (MPWR) detracted from performance. The stock declined following a report which suggested that the company's power management chips would not meet the requirements for use in NVIDIA chips.

An underweight position in Coinbase Global, Inc. (COIN) detracted from performance this quarter. The stock appreciated in November following the election of Donald Trump, as cryptocurrency values increased. The rise in crypto prices boosted Coinbase's performance, given its role as a leading platform for trading and exchanging digital currencies.

An overweight position in Builders FirstSource, Inc. (BLDR) detracted from performance. The company's stock was negatively impacted by challenging market conditions in the housing sector, driven primarily by rising bond yields, which have increased borrowing costs and dampened demand in the market.

Current strategy and outlook

After months of noise surrounding the U.S. presidential election, markets have now refocused on macroeconomic data, which offer mixed signals. Key concerns include global geopolitical tensions, especially around tariffs and deteriorating sentiment tied to mega-cap positioning and broader market weakness. Despite these challenges, U.S. equities should continue to benefit from robust consumer spending, optimism around AI and solid corporate earnings. U.S. inflation is projected at 2.5% for 2025, but the new administration's policies could reverse the disinflation trend.

Holdings detail

Companies mentioned in this report percentage of portfolio investments, as of 12/31/24: AppLovin Corp. 4.06%, Astera Labs, Inc. 1.11%, Nu Holdings Ltd. 0.00%, Monolithic Power Systems, Inc. 1.98%, Coinbase Global, Inc. 0.62% and Builders FirstSource, Inc. 1.09%; 0% indicates that the security is no longer in the portfolio. Portfolio holdings are subject to daily change.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Murphy USA: Good Fundamentals Reflected In Valuation (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Murphy USA (NYSE:MUSA) have been a solid performer over the past year, adding 11%, as the owner of gas stations and convenience stores continues to churn out solid results. While shares have pulled back from their late 2024 highs like most stocks, MUSA has held in fairly over the volatile past week, given its business is fairly isolated from tariff pressures. I last covered shares in May 2024, rating MUSA a buy, and since then, the stock has added a strong 14% while the market has shed 4%, making now a good time to revisit MUSA.

Murphy USA is a leading owner of gas stations and associated convenience stores. In total, the company operates over 1,700 locations in 27 states, primarily in the Sun Belt and Midwest. Over the long-term, it has been a steady generator of free cash flow, and it has aggressively returned capital to investors while also growing organically. Since 2013, it has allocated 47% of cash flow to buybacks, cutting its share count by 57%. At the same time, thanks to its investments in modernizing its stores, its loyalty programs, and expansion efforts, its volume growth has considerably outperformed the market.

Ultimately, gasoline is a very low growth market, but it also has limited cyclicality, which is why shares have held up fairly well during the recent spike in volatility. Outside of COVID and its unique economic shutdown, gasoline demand, while clearly exhibiting seasonal patterns, has had fairly modest economic sensitivity. That said, fuel demand is lower than a decade ago.

Now, I do view gasoline as a market in secular decline, though it is a very slowly melting ice cube, rather than a sharp collapse. There, of course, is the growth of electric vehicles (EV), which reduces gasoline consumption. That said, EV penetration growth has slowed. Still, thanks to advances in engine technology, new cars are simply more fuel efficient than ten years ago. All else equal, it is likely that the gasoline-consuming cars built in 5 and 10 years from now will also be somewhat more fuel efficient. As the US auto fleet rolls over and new cars replace older vehicles on the road, we will see ongoing gradual fuel consumption declines.

Indeed in the company’s fourth quarter, Murphy USA reported that fuel volumes were flat at 4.8 billion gallons in 2024 but down 1.1% on a same-store basis. While it can likely keep aggregate sales flattish via new store growth, I expect same-store sales to decline 0.5-1.25% annually over the long run.

Now, in the near-term, I am slightly more optimistic. President Trump has implemented a 25% tariff on imported cars, which will likely make new cars more expensive and reduce the pace of sales all else equal. This will slow the transformation of the fleet and reduce the headwind from improved fuel efficiency. More meaningfully, tariff uncertainty has caused oil and gasoline prices to plunge. As you can see below, gasoline prices have fallen by 27% from a year ago.

This decline is well-timed ahead of the critical summer driving season and may lead to some more driving-based vacations over the summer. To the extent tariffs are inflationary, they will impact goods prices much more than services prices—the cost of a night at a hotel room will be relatively unimpacted. As such, I can envision consumers shifting their discretionary budgets away from goods and towards services, which should be a net positive for domestic gasoline demand.

Additionally, sudden falls in wholesale prices tend to be good for MUSA’s margins. In general, prices at the pump move a bit more slowly than at the wholesale level, so quick plunges cause temporary margin gains while spikes tend to compress margins. Over time, prices at the pump converge, but this will be a favorable tailwind for Q2 results. In 2024, its margin on each gallon of fuel compressed to 30.5cpg (cents per gallon) from 31.4 in 2023. In Q4, it was stable at 32.5cpg aided by the sharp decline you can see above in September. I expect this decline in gasoline prices, should it persist, to lift results in the May-July months.

While known for its gas stations, its convenience stores are the key source of profits for the company. Of course, stopping for gas is the impetus for most customers to walk into the convenience store, so the two businesses are inextricably linked, but much of the profit comes from getting the consumer out of the car and into the store, which is why MUSA has invested in improving the cleanliness and quality of its layouts.

We are seeing results come through. In its most recent quarter, merchandise dollars rose by 5.6% to $209 million with margins expanding 50bps to 19.9%. Nicotine accounts for about 45% of merchandise sales. Both nicotine and non-nicotine were up about 3% on a same-store basis. Importantly, most convenience store products have limited tariff exposure, given the US is a net agricultural exporter. Additionally, like gasoline, c-store sales have proven to be largely recession-proof, even rising during the financial crisis.

I do not view the current economic uncertainty as likely to weigh on the company’s 2025 results. In 2025, MUSA is expecting $1-1.12 billion of EBITDA, up about 6% from 2024 levels at the midpoint. Given some gasoline margin expansion and store growth, I view this as a credible expectation. Last year, MUSA did 79 new stores, and it expects a similar total this year, though with a shift towards new stores, we should see a higher impact on sales. Even with a similar store count, it expected cap-ex to be down slightly.

This is one area where I do see risk. Given tariffs, construction inflation could be worse than we anticipated a few months ago. MUSA could respond to this pressure by slowing its pace of construction, but I expect cap-ex to come in at the high-end of its range. MUSA generated $390 million of 2024 free cash flow. I expect at least $400 million in 2025, given wider gasoline margins but some upside risk to cap-ex. Working capital may also be a modest headwind.

Importantly, Murphy is well positioned to return that cash flow to investors. It has an excellent balance sheet. With $1.8 billion of long-term debt, it has only 1.8x leverage. Given the stability of the business, MUSA could easily manage its business at 2.5x. I do not expect it to lever up, but given its strong balance sheet, we can expect all free cash flow to be focused on capital returns. In February, Murphy did increase its dividend by 2%, but that still leaves shares with just a 0.4% dividend yield. Instead, MUSA prioritizes buybacks, reducing its share count by nearly 5% in 2024. I expect at least 4% share count reduction in over the next twelve months.

When I last covered MUSA, I had targeted $450, with an upside case of $490, which shares briefly surpassed. After their recent pullback, shares sit at my prior target. With about $20-22 of go-forward free cash flow, MUSA has a 4.5%-5% free cash yield. I view that as appropriate. Ultimately, its core business has stable long-term prospects with modest C-store growth offsetting gasoline declines, and it grows its store count ~3% per year for about an 8% long-term return.

After significant outperformance, MUSA appears to be fairly reflecting its fundamentals. As such, I see shares as a “hold.” Now, if we do see the market fall further, I would expect MUSA to fall less and outperform, given its limited tariff exposure. Similarly, on a rebound, it would likely lag. Overall, I see MUSA as a “hold” and a stock that investors can “hide” in to wait out tariff volatility, but I no longer see shares positioned for ongoing long-term outperformance and would want a pullback closer to $400-420 before buying.

data.title: Sportradar Group AG: Business With Strong Structural Moat
data.description:
Investment summary

My recommendation for Sportradar Group AG (NASDAQ:SRAD) is a buy rating. I think this is a business with strong structural moats, and because of that, I believe it has real pricing power over time. There are also visible growth drivers that should continue to support growth in the coming years.

Business Overview

SRAD is a leading business-to-business (B2B) provider of sports data and content solutions. Its main customers are sports betting operators, media outlets, and sports organizations across the globe. SRAD’s key solutions include real-time live data and odds, streaming and betting engagement, managed betting services, marketing technology, and integrity services. To give a sense of SRAD’s scale in this industry, in 2024 alone, it processed 87 billion betting selections and managed more than 65 million active betters across its Managed Trading Services [MTS] platform. It also enables the operations of >800 betting operators, 900 media companies, and 400 sports leagues and federations.

Competitive Advantage

SRAD is one of the businesses that, I believe, has a huge structural competitive advantage that is not easy to replicate.

To begin, SRAD is deeply integrated into the betting ecosystem, and it is in a position where its services and solutions are mission critical. If you look at the flowchart above, assuming SRAD doesn’t exist, each of the 800 betting operators and 900 media companies (operators) will have to liaise and connect to every individual sports league to gather the required data (there are tons of such data, by the way). It will be extremely inefficient, costly to manage, and filled with execution errors (too much data being exchanged with too many parties).

With SRAD, all of these inefficiencies go away. In fact, productivity across the value chain increases dramatically because resources can be best allocated to where each player in the value chain is good at—the sports league focuses on managing games, SRAD focuses on collecting, cleaning, and managing relationships with operators, and operators focus on capturing demand from end users. As you can imagine, SRAD becomes deeply embedded in the core workflows of operators as they rely heavily on SRAD to perform functions like trading and pricing, which are the core functions to facilitate their entire business. Once embedded, it becomes extremely sticky because of a few reasons:

  1. Operators do not want to risk operation failure during switching of providers.
  2. It could be very costly and extremely time-consuming to switch if the entire tech stack is already integrated with SRAD.
  3. At its core, data from sports leagues is a commodity, and if SRAD is already doing a great job (no hiccups or major errors), there is very little value in switching providers.

The other competitive advantage is how modular the SRAD platform is, which makes it incredibly attractive to operators. Think about it, if you are an operator, instead of using several point solutions that are costly, difficult to integrate (APIs unlikely to work well together), and hard to manage (different vendors), you can just use SRAD to settle all your problems.

Because of all these, I think SRAD has real pricing power over time. As long as SRAD does not mis-execute and continues to expand its product suite (like MTS—a risk management tool—that has reached EUR35 billion in sales), it is in a very good position to continue raising prices. And I think its customers will be very willing to absorb such price increases vs. the risk of disrupting a critical part of their infrastructure.

Growth Drivers and Outlook

Looking forward, I see three structural growth drivers: (1) deeper monetization through take-rate expansion and product adoption; (2) geographic expansion, especially in Brazil; and (3) AI innovations.

For point one, as of FY24, only 40% of SRAD clients used >four products, and ~60% used three or fewer. As I said above, I think there is a lot of value for operators to consolidate their vendors and just use SRAD as a one-stop shop, and this has huge upside potential for SRAD. Per management’s math, the difference in monetization (revenue multiple effect) of a customer using 3 products vs. >6x is more than 7x difference (14.2/2).

For point two, SRAD is still very much focused on penetrating international markets. The traction is real, as can be seen with the progress in Brazil, where SRAD is now serving 50 operators and has signed 35 sportsbooks for MTS services. More notably, these international expansion efforts are translating into actual revenue growth. SRAD’s rest of world revenue growth has accelerated from 18% y/y in FY23 to 20% in FY24.

For the last point, this is hard to quantify and probably still in the early innings. I think SRAD AI-led innovation could help to unlock new revenue streams. One example is the 4Sight streaming platform. According to management, stream viewers spend 24% more time on average compared to standard streams. This is huge because it means more fan engagement, which means more betting opportunities, and that is revenue potential for operators. This should justify SRAD charging a fee for it.

Valuation

In my model, I have SRAD generating ~EUR438 million in adj. EBITDA in FY27, driven by 15% revenue growth CAGR and adj. EBITDA margin expanding to 26% in FY27. I believe my growth estimates are relatively conservative considering that previous years’ growth was >20% and there are visible growth drivers. For margins, I expect margins to continue expanding since this is a business with very high incremental margins. For reference, in 4Q24, incremental margins were 39%. That said, I am not assuming multiples to go higher from here. It is most likely going to stay flat as the market offsets the negative impact of lower growth by the positive impact from improving margins.

Risk

The biggest risk factor, in my view, is the macro environment. The entire value chain is supported by consumers willing to bet, and that is a discretionary expense. There is sufficient macro data (poor consumer confidence, retail spending falling, etc.) that suggest consumers are cutting back on spending. If end users decide to cut back on betting, this will impact SRAD indirectly, given its variable revenue model (depends on operator’s gross gaming revenue).

Conclusion

My view for SRAD is a buy rating. SRAD's structural moat is strong, the upsell opportunity is clear, and execution on expanding internationally has been solid. If SRAD continues to grow as guided and expand its adj. EBITDA margin, I believe the upside is attractive even without multiples inflecting upwards.

data.title: DEM: High-Dividend Emerging Market Stocks Now Under 8x Earnings
data.description:
Emerging market equities have outperformed US stocks so far in 2025, and the high dividend yield factor, along with minimum volatility, has produced alpha in what has turned out to be a volatile year so far. About 12 months ago, I analyzed the WisdomTree Emerging Markets High Dividend Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:DEM). I upgraded shares to a buy based on its valuation and a strong chart, but there are now some changes.

Shares rallied nicely through the following September, capped by monster gains out of China toward the end of last year's third quarter. A strong US dollar and steep US alpha stymied EM outperformance opportunities, though. And while DEM has beaten the SPX year to date, the fund is down 5.6% (total return) since my April 2024 write-up.

I reiterate a buy rating. The valuation remains extremely compelling, and I will note important areas on the chart to monitor in a very uncertain global macro environment.

EM Equities Underperforming the S&P 500 (SPY) YoY, Outperforming YTD

According to WisdomTree, DEM seeks to track the price and yield performance, before fees and expenses, of the WisdomTree Emerging Markets High Dividend Index. The ETF allows investors to gain exposure to EM equities across the market cap spectrum, with an emphasis on high dividend yield companies. The fund can be used to diversify geographical and style exposure.

DEM is a medium-sized ETF with $2.6 billion in assets under management as of April 7, 2025. That’s down from $2.8 billion from early Q2 last year. Its annual expense ratio is to the high side at 63 basis points, while its trailing 12-month dividend yield is very high at 6.1%--about twice that of a broad EM fund.

Share-price momentum has rolled over lately—the ETF Grade in that category is exceptionally weak at a D, per Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. Its realized volatility is elevated today, but the Risk rating is actually not that bad—I expect it to weaken as high standard deviation trends lately seep into the quant rating. Finally, liquidity is healthy—average daily volume is north of 300,000 shares, and the 30-day median bid/ask spread is 7 basis points, per the issuer.

For an update on the portfolio, the 4-star ETF by Morningstar plots in the upper-right section of the style box, indicating its bent to large-cap value. That differs from today’s emerging market landscape which has a larger weight to the growth style.

Being large-cap value, its price-to-earnings ratio is under 9x, which is very a compelling coupled with the high yield. WisdomTree notes that the forward earnings multiple is 7.6x as of April 8 (pictured below). Flip that around, and the earnings yield is more than 13%.

DEM: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

WisdomTree Funds Valuations

So, what drives the high yield? Financials is the biggest weight at almost 28%. Energy, another high payout sector, is a large 14% holding of DEM. Energy, however, has been among the badly hit corners of the global stock market during the post-Liberation Day sell-off.

Information Technology is a 11% position, a significant underweight compared to its stake in the S&P 500. Prospective investors should acknowledge that DEM has very high cyclical exposure, which can add to the risk profile—there isn’t much access to growth or defensives.

From a country perspective, China is the biggest weight at 25.1%.

DEM: Holdings & Dividend Information

Seasonally, we are in a rough spot, although DEM’s long-term performance has not been all that great as EM has been out of favor. Additionally, all of DEM’s long-term return has come via its yield, not price appreciation. That being said, the May through October 6-month stretch has been slightly bearish over the years on a price-only basis.

DEM: Weak Seasonal Trends Beyond April

The Technical Take

Technically, it’s not an attractive picture. Notice in the chart below that shares broke down from an uptrend earlier this year. Before that, DEM encountered a long-term downtrend line that stretched back many years. Today, the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph illustrates that the ETF is in deeply oversold conditions, suggesting that a bounce within a downtrend is quite likely soon. We could see the fund fall further to the next level of support, which, I believe, will come into play at the November 2023 low just above $36.

Also, take a look at the volume by price indicator on the left side of the graph—I suspect that the rally attempt will now be made tough considering the ample amount of supply all the way up to the mid-40s. As it stands, more than half of the run-up from the last few years has been retraced, and the bears are gaining control of the primary trend—that's evidenced by a now-falling long-term 200-day moving average.

DEM: Uptrend Broken, Eyeing Support Near $36

The Bottom Line

While the technicals are not encouraging, DEM’s valuation is very attractive. The high-yield stems from large allocations to Financials and Energy—a pair of cyclical sectors—which is a risk. The earnings yield above 13% is too cheap to bypass, however.

data.title: Despite Being Bullish On Tesla, I Think TSLY Is A Yield Trap
data.description:
The YieldMax TSLA Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:TSLY) attracts a lot of interest because of its high-yielding monthly income (a nearly 80% distribution rate, according to the fund, and the most recent dividend was $0.4638/share). But honestly, it looks like a yield trap. Don't fall for it.

The way the TSLY ETF is structured — it seeks to make income from employing options strategies on Tesla (TSLA) stock — sets it up for poor total returns relative to Tesla stock.

TSLY generally underperforms greatly when TSLA stock rises, while seeing almost as much downside as TSLA when it falls. The risk/reward ratio is against you, and I'll show examples of that later on.

Moreover, the dividend is highly unstable and can continue falling over time due to NAV erosion unless TSLA stock rallies. But if you think TSLA will rally, you'd be better off buying the stock directly.

I'm actually bullish on Tesla stock, as I gave it a Buy rating last month after a big sell-off. I even mentioned a TSLA options strategy that can give you a great risk/reward ratio (unlike TSLY), so I recommend reading my bullish thesis on Tesla.

Since I'm bullish on Tesla, I expect this ETF to rise in the short term as a result. However, I simply don't like this fund's strategy. These two factors balance out, leading me to a "Hold" rating. But as I said, buying TSLA itself, either through shares or the options strategy I laid out, is much better.

How Does The TSLY ETF Work?

The fund's strategy involves several parts. First, TSLY doesn't own Tesla shares directly. Instead, it generally takes a synthetic long position in the stock. This means that it buys at-the-money (ATM) call options on TSLA and simultaneously sells ATM puts with the same strike price and expiration. It's called a synthetic long position because it basically mimics Tesla stock's performance without having to buy shares.

But mimicking Tesla's performance isn't enough, as it needs to generate income for investors. Therefore, to generate income, TSLY will either employ a covered call strategy (selling an OTM call) or a covered call credit spread strategy (selling an OTM call while buying an OTM call of the same expiration but an even higher strike, for a net credit). The OTM calls generally have expirations of 1 month or less.

When the fund thinks TSLA stock will rebound sharply, it may shift to the covered call spread strategy rather than the covered call in order to limit losses on the short call if TSLA spikes.

This lowers the income the fund collects, but the idea is to limit losses and provide extra upside.

The rest of TSLY's assets are generally held in short-term U.S. Treasuries and cash. These positions provide collateral for the options positions and also generate some extra income.

Everything I just described can be found in the screenshot I included below from the fund's prospectus.

The fund continuously rolls its options, meaning contracts are replaced as they expire.

What's Wrong With TSLY?

The bad thing about TSLY is that it underperforms TSLA significantly on the upswing, while still performing poorly on the downswing. During strong rallies, TSLY underperforms because the short calls it sells cap the upside. The synthetic long increases in value, but if TSLY sells a $260 strike call, for example, there's no more upside if TSLA goes over $260.

Meanwhile, when Tesla stock is falling, the income collected from selling calls isn't enough to offset the heavy losses — at least it hasn't been in the past.

Here's an example. The chart below starts from December 17, 2024, when TSLA stock topped. The candlesticks represent TSLY's performance, while the blue and red bars represent TSLA, and the results are adjusted for dividends. This downturn led to a 46.38% drop in TSLY and a 50.98% drop in TSLA. That's hardly any better than TSLA's performance.

The extra income generated from options selling didn't shield the downside enough, especially relative to how much upside it caps on the way up. Below is a chart of TSLY vs. TSLA on the upswing from October 11, 2024, to December 17, 2024. TSLA returned 117.99%, while TSLY returned 80.19%.

This strategy hasn't done well in the longer term, either. Since inception, TSLY has returned a dismal -12.46%, including dividends, while TSLA has gained 32.66%.

It seems to me that the fund has almost all the downside of owning TSLA stock while having capped upside. Owning it makes little sense to me.

The situation isn't much better when Tesla trades sideways. Below is a chart from June 12, 2023, to September 30, 2024, when TSLA stock returned 0.86%, but TSLY returned 6.9% after factoring in dividends.

Why Are The Dividends Falling?

People buy TSLY for its dividend, but the dividend has been falling. Its July 2023 distribution was $2.1322 per share, but the most recent one was $0.4638 per share. The dividends vary month by month, but the trend has generally been lower, even though TSLA stock has risen since TSLY's inception. Why, though?

I suspect a big reason is the fund's NAV decline. You can see how its share price keeps declining when the chart isn't adjusted for dividends.

The fund basically pays all its income as dividends, meaning it doesn't retain anything. And as its NAV drops (especially if TSLA falls), it has fewer funds to invest, and less funds invested means shrinking dividends.

Next, when Tesla's OTM calls are relatively expensive, the fund might switch to its call spread strategy rather than covered calls. That's because when calls are expensive, it can mean the market anticipates TSLA to rise. TSLY's fund managers might think the same and employ the call spread strategy to help capture some upside potential and reduce losses from the short call. However, this strategy lowers the income the fund can collect, reducing the dividend.

If Tesla's stock price stays depressed, I'm almost certain TSLY's next dividend will be depressed relative to past payouts.

Would You Be Better Off Just Buying Tesla Stock?

I think if you're bullish on Tesla, you should just buy Tesla stock. If you're not bullish on TSLA and are looking for income from this ETF, I don't think its strategy is great. You'd be better off employing your own options strategies on TSLA (if you have experience with options trading), which would give you more control.

In addition, TSLY's high payout is hard to maintain due to the factors I mentioned earlier. It's not steady income.

The Bottom Line On TSLY

TSLY is an interesting ETF in theory, but in practice, its performance has been poor. It underperformed TSLA notably in sideways and uptrending markets, while it hardly outperformed during downtrends (you'd still be down a lot). Importantly, its dividend payout keeps trending lower, and this can continue unless TSLA stock rebounds. But as I said earlier, if you think TSLA will rebound, you might as well trade TSLA and/or its options directly.

Unless you have a very specific, short-term outlook and are taking a tactical trade on TSLY, the ETF probably isn't a good choice for most investors. It looks like a yield trap to me.

data.title: JFR: Tactical Buy After The Storm (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

The Nuveen Floating Rate Income Fund (NYSE:JFR) is a fixed income closed end fund we last covered at the beginning of the year on the back of a corporate action. At the time, we warned investors that a period of weakness was coming due to the structural mechanics of a rights offering. The CEF stabilized when the corporate action ended in February 2025, but has been recently sold-off significantly on the back of 'tariff Armageddon'.

In today's article we are going to explore JFR's valuation in light of the recent market action, and outline why the CEF is oversold on a short-term basis and thus represents a tactical 'Buy' opportunity.

Analytics first

  • AUM: $1.2 billion.
  • Sharpe Ratio: 0.1 (3Y).
  • Std. Deviation: 6.7 (3Y).
  • Yield: 13.5%
  • Premium/Discount to NAV: -12%
  • Z-Stat: -4
  • Leverage Ratio: 35%.
  • Composition: Leveraged Loans

A leveraged take on floating rate loans

JFR is a CEF which focuses on floating rate loans rated below investment grade:

  • Senior Loans: 85.8%
  • Cash: 11.9%
  • Bonds: 11%
  • Other: 1%

Kindly note the CEF reports its exposures including the leverage. Also note that the rights offering was a smart move in today's environment since it allowed the CEF to increase its cash balance without incurring any loss crystallization via collateral sales.

The fund is overweight single-B names, which represent more than 54% of the fund:

  • BBB: 8.6%
  • BB: 9.5%
  • B: 54.1%
  • CCC: 4.7%
  • Not Rated: 2.8%

The fund therefore is expected to have a lower NAV and price when single-B and double-BB spreads move up. This is exactly what has happened in the past month.

Credit is finally moving

In the past month, we have finally seen a move up in credit spreads, from historic lows:

We are using corporate bonds single-B spreads as a proxy for leveraged loans here. The above is the ICE BofA single-B US High Yield Index Option-Adjusted Spread, which is currently sitting at 4.69%. Kindly note, in February 2025 this figure was 2.65%. The move up in spreads has been swift and violent.

While we are not at the highs seen in 2022, i.e. 500 bps and above, we are nearing that figure. What is shocking is the speed of the move, having happened in virtually a month, while the economic backdrop outside tariffs remains the same. Unemployment is still low, and GDP growth is still robust. The credit markets, however, cannot ignore the tariff impact and the associated uncertainty.

JFR has been walloped: both in NAV and market price

Firstly, let us look at how JFR has done in the past month:

When comparing JFR to a cohort of peers, we can notice that Invesco Senior Income Trust (VVR) is the outlier in terms of performance. We have a piece out on that name and the drivers, so if interested, you should read that. JFR's move is virtually in line with the Eaton Vance Floating Rate Income Trust (EFT). As a CEF, Pioneer Floating Rate Trust (PHD) did the best. We also introduced a non-leveraged component, namely Invesco Senior Loan ETF (BKLN) for comparison purposes. BKLN is down a mere -3.73% here. Leverage delivers magnified returns, both on the upside and downside.

While JFR's move in not the worst, kindly keep in mind the CEF was already trading at a discount to NAV given its corporate action:

The rights offering moved the CEF to a slight discount to NAV, only to see that figure really widen out in the recent market move to close to -12%. This is the bottom of its historic range on a decade long look-back. We are of the opinion this is an extreme move given that economic fundamentals are still strong.

Let us look at the first chart from a NAV perspective now:

Now NAVs have not moved that much in the past month. In fact, the NAV moves for the CEFs are in line with the unleveraged ETF. Most of the price action has come from the significant widening of the discounts to NAV.

Is the discount to NAV justified? How about the yield?

From a distribution standpoint, approximately 82% of the yield is supported by cash-flow, while the rest is return of capital. Rates are undoubtedly going down, which will result in further pressure on distributions. Given the recent market turmoil, traders have now yet again increased the projected Fed Funds cuts for 2025 to an astounding five:

Is this correct? Will it happen? We do not know. Nobody knows, in fact. What we know for sure is that rates are moving down, and JFR will need to adjust its distribution. Let us be very conservative here and pencil-in a sustainable distribution after a 100 bps in Fed Funds cuts of roughly 10%, from the current 13.5%.

What we are looking at therefore is a CEF with a rough 10% yield (covered) after all said and done, an instrument which is trading at the bottom of the range in terms of discount when looking back ten years. Are defaults still low? You bet. Is the economy still doing ok? Absolutely.

Yes, the market is forward-looking, but right now, all possible bad news are priced in. And we include in the term 'bad news' the following: much lower Fed Funds, a higher default rate, higher unemployment. In our view, JFR is now slightly oversold, with a tariff normalization brining about a +5% move from the discount to NAV moving up and a slight NAV recovery.

While not apples for apples given some NAV slippage, also note that JFR's price is now close to its Covid levels:

Tactical trade versus outright buy and hold

We are of the opinion that JFR is set to bounce from oversold conditions, and just like we did for VVR, we are of the opinion that a tactical trade here can be done. We are targeting a +5% bounce from oversold conditions that embed an Armageddon market valuation. Some investors can tactically trade around a position given market metrics. We are one of them. We hold JFR but will trade around position size on this market move.

Conclusion

JFR is a leveraged loan fund. The name saw a rights offering earlier in the year which increased its cash position (a defensive stance), but has been creamed by the recent market action despite a sticky NAV. The CEF is now trading at a -12% discount to NAV, the bottom of its historic range. We are a tactical 'Buy' here for the name, aiming for a +5% bounce from deeply oversold conditions.

data.title: Voya Infrastructure, Industrials And Materials Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Strategy overview

The Fund seeks potential enhanced stability of returns over a market cycle through investments in global companies with potential to benefit from increased infrastructure spending, and by selling call options on select equity Indexes or exchange-traded funds.

Key takeaways

• During the quarter, the Fund benefited from strong stock selection, particularly within the United States and within the utilities, industrials, materials and information technology sectors.

• U.S. stocks remain attractive driven by post-election optimism, investment, productivity gains from deregulation, innovation and energy independence.

• Outside of the U.S., pockets of weakness in China and the eurozone have become more apparent.

Portfolio review

For the quarter, the Fund provided a total return of 3.94% on a net asset value basis, and a total return of the Voya Infrastructure Industrials and Materials Custom Index, returned 6.78%.

Equity portfolio

For the reporting period, the equity sleeve of the Fund outperformed its reference Index, the Voya Infrastructure Industrials and Materials Custom Index, due mainly to a positive impact from the core model where five of five pillars were positive. Stock selection effects were another contributor to performance. On the regional level portfolio holdings in the U.S. contributed the most while portfolio holdings in Japan were the largest detractors.

At the sector level stock selection in utilities, industrials, materials, and information technology had a positive impact on performance. Among the key contributors was an overweight position in Delta Air Lines, Inc. (DAL), Entergy Corp. (ETR) and F5, Inc (FFIV).

Conversely, stock selection in communication services and energy detracted from performance. At the stock level, the underweight positions in Honeywell International Inc. (HON), Boeing Co. (BA) and Williams Co, Inc (WMB).

Option portfolio

During the quarter, the Fund's option strategy had a positive impact on returns. The covered call strategy seeks to generate premiums and retain some potential for upside appreciation. The Fund implemented this strategy by typically writing call options on sector and regional exchange-traded funds (ETFs), the selection and allocation of which resulted from an optimization intended to closely track the Fund's reference Index. The strike prices of the options written were typically at or near the money, with expiration dates around one month at inception.

Current strategy and outlook

Global challenges and opportunities confront the markets in 2025. However, the U.S. remains attractive driven by post-election optimism, investment, productivity gains from deregulation, innovation and energy independence. Despite some softening, the U.S. labor market remains resilient. Inflation is expected to stay manageable, allowing the U.S. Federal Reserve to continue its gradual easing.

Outside the U.S., pockets of weakness are becoming more apparent, particularly in the eurozone and China.

The eurozone faces cooling services and ongoing manufacturing challenges, heightening economic uncertainty. In China, new policies aim to address economic sluggishness, especially in the property market, and boost investor confidence. However, the investment environment looks more promising in Japan, where deflation has been curbed and corporate reforms have made progress. Despite this, there has been relatively little foreign investment, creating an opportunity as more investors recognize the growth story.

Holdings detail

Companies mentioned in this report percentage of portfolio investments, as of 12/31/24: Delta Air Lines, Inc. 0.67%, Entergy Corp. 0.98%, F5, Inc. 0.80%, Honeywell International Inc. 0.36%, Boeing Co. 0.39% and Williams Co, Inc. 0.51; 0% indicates that the security is no longer in the portfolio. Portfolio holdings are subject to change daily.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
